# Transcrição: 08-Complementary Products - Foundr Premium

- **Fonte:** 08-Complementary Products - Foundr Premium
- **Duração:** 09:10
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Complementary products are those products that you can sell to existing audience and they can also be looked at as either cross sells or upsells. So a complementary product could be that you're selling product X and product Y is closely related to that. So once they've bought product X you can email them and try to get them to buy product Y. You're letting the customer get their product, get comfortable with the product and then saying hey you like product X from us, we've got this really cool product Y, why don't you check that out and then they come back to your website and they like what you've done, they like how it's delivered, they like the quality, the branding and then they buy that product from you and then you've got this kind of customer brand relationship where they're coming back because they trust you to continuously buy from you. And then the other kind of complementary products that I really like are cross sells and upsells. So example of cross sell and upsell, if you're looking at say our product, our quad lock, if you buy it for your motorcycle and it comes with a standard blue lever, you might have a black or red or different color bike and you want to change that lever. As you check out it'll say do you want to upgrade to a black lever or a white lever or red lever and you'll be adding that to your cart and it's sort of upgrading your kit so we call that an upsell. They generally happen during the checkout process of your onboarding product and they're awesome because you can get really good margins even when your basic numbers of what you buy it for and what you sell it for don't look as good as an onboarding product because you don't have any consumer acquisition cost in that. It's literally the customers here, they came for product X and then you're tacking on product Y to that sale. Customers already paid for with product X, you're just getting that little bit extra in your cart and it's a great way of getting your average order value up. It's a good way to originally approach a complimentary product or even find a complimentary product is look at what your customer is doing and what they're probably doing after they leave your store. So if you're selling widget X, which for us could be that we're selling them a smartphone case but a smartphone case that goes on their bike, could it be that actually one of our other onboarding products could be a complimentary product to the sale of that because they may have a motorcycle, they may have a car so we can then take that product and offer it to that customer who's come and onboarded with it with the flagship product and turn that customer into a complimentary product buying customer and that way you get a much greater customer lifetime value by offering them additional products that are more likely in their wheelhouse to buy because they've already dealt with you on one thing and they're probably happy with you and they respect the brand more and they're looking if anything for more reasons to do business with you.

## [03:02 - 06:02]

Another thing to keep in mind when you're looking at especially like cross-sell up-sell or even any complimentary product, especially cross-sell and up-sell products, these are products that is really not an issue if it's a Me Too product because you don't have to be reinventing the wheel, you don't have to have something completely innovative to capture that customer. Sometimes you've captured that customer with a flagship onboarding, maybe an innovative product and then you can then offer them those Me Too products and like we said to the Me Too products it comes back to the brand and that they trust your brand and that you can provide them that widget whatever it is that service and they're getting it from you as opposed to someone else because they trust what you've done with them in the previous sales. Okay, so now we're going to look at the difference in the pricing structure and the cost makeup of a flagship onboarding product versus a complimentary either up-sell, cross-sell, sell type product. Okay, so first we're going to look at the onboarding product, we'll call it product X. Now product X has a retail price of $100. The product costs $15 to make, $5 landing cost, $10 to pick pack and ship and the customer acquisition cost is $40 and we also give this customer onboarding product 10% discount so the margin is $20 or as we call it 20% margin. Okay, so now we sell that same customer who bought product X onboarding product, we sell them product Y which is a complimentary product and it's an up-sell, cross-sell type arrangement so we have that customer decides to also purchase product Y and has a retail price of $30 and a buy price is $5 for us and the landing cost is $2.50. There's no pick pack shipping or acquisition cost within this sale because all of that is covered by the acquisition cost and the shipping cost that was incurred on the original onboarding product. So one of the first came to your website to buy and we'll also give them the 10% discount of $3 so the total landing cost is $10.50. So now when we look at that, we now have a margin, a dollar margin of $19.50 or a percentage margin of 65% on that complimentary product. So in this scenario with the complimentary product, you're actually making more margin on the complimentary product than you are from the onboarding product, from the product that user first came to your website to buy so it's a really powerful way to up your AOV and make more dollars per customer. And another really good reason for complimentary products at this part of your selling cycle is that it actually gives you more headroom, more money to spend on requiring your customer because even if you only break even on that first time buy, these complimentary is where you get your lifetime value and where the business model can sometimes start to make sense. So now with your new knowledge between onboarding products and flagship products and complimentary products, it's a really good time to look at it and go, okay, so onboarding products,

## [06:02 - 09:03]

we have all the customer acquisition costs going to a new market, all the learning, complimentary products at this point in time are actually fairly cheap and easy to get off the ground and it's just going to increase your AOV and get you more dollars per sale that you can then spend on marketing and getting new customers in the door. So one of the best tools to get your complimentary product sales going is your email and this is why it's so important to try and get all your customers' emails that you can and try and really respect them and keep on the level where you can continuously email them so you don't want to spam them, you want to give them stuff of value and if you don't have something of value, I tend to not give them anything and wait until I do have something of value because at the end of the day, email is just so cheap to run, it's not like running ads on Facebook where often complimentary products are not as expensive so if you start running ads to sell complimentary products and the customer acquisition chews into that, you may not have the margin there where an email is effectively free to send, you send them that email, they click on that link, they come back and they buy and you've almost got a customer acquisition of zero. So over the years we've had different email templates and different email flows that we send out but the current flow that we send out is based around a big customer survey where we understand if customer opts into our onboarding product of X, they're more likely to buy Y and understanding that meant we knew exactly which emails to send people and which order and the other thing we've done over time is better understand exactly which markets, how long it takes for them to get their product so we know when to time that second or third sort of follow up email which isn't anything about the order delivery or the order confirmation, it is you've got your product, hopefully by now you're stoked with it, how about you check out this other product. So the timing is really important here and another really important fact is it's not all just in the email you're sending, it's what you've done either before or after that so to make this email effective when you're going to follow up with them, you want to hope that they've had a good experience with the delivery and the tracking and all this because the better experience they have the more likely it is that this email is going to work. There's a lot of smart things you can do in the email but if you gave them a bad experience before the email, this follow up email is not going to land as well as it would. Now over the years we've changed a lot of different ways we've done emails from something that looks really slick to almost looks part of the website and then back to something that looks a lot more like just an email you could get someone, a bit of a logo to get the trust so they remember you, just one image and then really clean, clear call to actions and we often do if we've got some kind of discount code in there that'll be like a noxious sort of red color, so it really stands out and the next thing is that we always do these days is on the buy button we actually have the action that we want the user to take when they click that button so if you look at this one here it'll be like buy, buy, come out now. There's really basic, really good things that have just served us quite well over the years.
